reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No forbidden patterns: no hand-authored YAML config, no StacktapeConfig type imports, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig — all appropriate for a Console UI page.
The Info callout explicitly separates AWS infrastructure costs from Stacktape's subscription fee, directly addressing a common first-time-user confusion.

Trend-label table ('+X%', '-X%', 'Flat', 'New spend', 'No spend') is source-verified against the OverviewPage.tsx trendLabel computation logic and gives the reader concrete reference for what they'll see.
'Other' grouping behaviour for >10 projects is correctly documented and confirmed by `sorted.slice(10)` in CostsByProjectChart.

The time-range selector caveat — that it controls deployment stats only, not cost views — is clearly stated and supported by the source (useCostsData fetches current month/year independently of the timeRange state).
Warning callout about the 24-hour delay is accurately sourced from the noDataMessage in CostsAndUsagePage.tsx.
Per-page pagination of 10 and the default sort-by-cost-descending are confirmed by `perPageAmount={10}` and `defaultSortColumnIndex={3}` in the source.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, comparison/choosing) and answers are concise and self-contained.
Cross-page linking to Budgets, Per-resource breakdown, and Optimization tips clearly scopes this page to 'viewing' only, as required by section instructions.
problems: 'A sparkline below the card shows the last 6 months of total spend' — the sparkline data comes from `trpc.overviewCostTrend` whose server-side implementation is not in the provided sources. The claim of '6 months' is unverified and may be wrong.

'Providing roughly 24 months of history' is inaccurate. The `getSelectableMonths()` function returns (current month index + 1) months of the current year plus 12 months of the previous year — between 13 months (January) and 24 months (December). At most months of the year this is closer to 18-20, not '24'. The FAQ repeats the same incorrect figure.

The 'Per-stage cost detail' section — including column names 'Resource name', 'Type', 'Costs' and the 'four decimal places' precision claim — has no corresponding source file provided. The navigate target `/projects/${stage}/costs` is confirmed but the destination page's column layout is unverified, making this section speculation.

The dashboard is rendered via `DashboardGrid` with `defaultLayout` per-gadget properties, strongly implying a draggable/resizable layout that users can customise. The page never mentions this, leaving a new user confused when they can drag gadgets around.
optionalImprovements: Fix the '6 months' sparkline claim: either omit the month count ('A sparkline shows recent monthly spend totals') or note it is determined server-side and may vary.

Correct the '~24 months of history' claim to 'the current year's months plus the full previous calendar year — up to about 24 months depending on when in the year you are accessing the page'. Update the FAQ answer for 'How far back can I view cost history?' to match.
Add a one-sentence note that the overview dashboard layout is customisable — gadgets can be rearranged and resized — so users aren't confused when they first encounter the drag handles.

The per-stage cost detail column descriptions ('Resource name', 'Type', 'Costs', four decimal places) should either be confirmed from source or softened to 'columns vary; click any stack row to explore the per-resource breakdown' until the StackCostsPage source can be reviewed.

The Budget Alerts panel description could note that clicking a budget navigates to '/budgets' (confirmed in source via `navigate('/budgets')` in BudgetStatusPanel) for readers who want to know what happens before clicking.

Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.

problems: FACTUAL ERROR (mandatory fix): 'roughly 24 months of history' appears in both the Filters section and the FAQ, but `getSelectableMonths()` in CostsAndUsagePage.tsx generates current-year months to date plus all 12 months of the previous year. For May 2026 that is 17 months, not ~24. The maximum (~24) is only reached in December.

UNGROUNDED CONTENT: The 'Per-stage cost detail' section describes specific column names ('Resource name', 'Type', 'Costs'), a total at the top of the table, and four-decimal-place cost display for a sub-page whose source code (`/projects/${name}/${stage}/costs`) is not in the provided source files. These details cannot be verified and may be speculative.

NAMING INCONSISTENCY: The gadget is titled 'AWS Costs by Resource' in the source (`{ id: 'costs-resource', title: 'AWS Costs by Resource' }`), but the page calls it 'Costs by resource type' in the section heading and in the FAQ answer. The inconsistency will confuse users looking at the actual Console.

UNCONFIRMED CLAIM: 'A sparkline below the card shows the last 6 months of total spend' — the source uses `costTrendQuery.data?.months` but the number of months returned by the `overviewCostTrend` tRPC endpoint is not visible in the provided source. '6 months' cannot be confirmed.

MINOR UNCONFIRMED CLAIM in FAQ: 'Dashboard data refreshes each time you load the page.' React Query default stale/refetch behavior isn't specified in the provided source; this may mislead users who expect a manual refresh button.
mandatoryFixes: Fix 'roughly 24 months of history' in both the Filters section and the FAQ. The `getSelectableMonths()` function in CostsAndUsagePage.tsx produces months from 1 through the current month of the current year, plus all 12 months of the previous year. The correct description is: 'the current month plus all months of the previous calendar year' (between 13 and 24 months depending on when in the year you are). Remove or correct the '~24 months' claim in both places it appears.
optionalImprovements: Either remove the 'Per-stage cost detail' section entirely (the source page is absent from the provided files) or add a caveat that the column structure shown is subject to change, and avoid claiming specific column names or decimal precision that can't be verified.
Rename the 'Costs by resource type' section heading and FAQ entry to match the source gadget title 'AWS Costs by Resource' to keep Console labels consistent with documentation.

Soften the 'dashboard data refreshes each time you load the page' FAQ answer to 'data is fetched when you navigate to the page' or remove the claim, since the React Query cache and stale-time config aren't visible in the provided source.

If the cost trend sparkline months count can be confirmed from the tRPC endpoint definition, replace the unconfirmed '6 months' claim with the verified number; otherwise soften to 'recent monthly spend totals' without stating a specific count.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page separates viewing costs from budgets and optimization, which matches the managing-costs section guidance.
The organization overview section correctly captures the current-month cost behavior and the fact that overview time-range tabs drive deployment stats, not cost data.
Trend labels, budget-alert display fields, the active AWS account filter, the monthly selector, table sort column, and 10-row pagination are mostly grounded in the provided Console source.
The FAQ is useful and spans Stacktape-specific, AWS-cost, and comparison questions rather than only repeating Console UI details.
problems: Several Console workflow claims are more specific than the source supports, including “accessible from the Console sidebar,” “Click any budget to navigate to the budgets management page,” and “Click any row to drill into that stage’s per-resource cost view.” The source shows route navigation for row actions and budgets, but not the user-facing sidebar or click labels.

The draft overstates cost-data provenance: “Both views pull from AWS Cost and Usage Reports” is not shown in the provided source. The Costs page source calls `awsAccountCostReports` and says cost reports are generated daily; the overview source does not identify AWS Cost and Usage Reports.

The “Costs by project” section is under-supported. The gadget title says “AWS Costs by Project,” but the implementation calls `overviewCostsByService`, uses variables named `services`, and does not prove the items are Stacktape projects. The draft should write more cautiously or align with the exact UI label only.
The draft claims the cost sparkline shows “the last 6 months of total spend,” but the source only maps `overviewCostTrend.data?.months`; no source-provided count is visible.

The FAQ says dashboards show all AWS costs in the connected account and that untagged/account-level charges may not appear in the per-stack view. The provided source only shows attributed stacks from a cost report breakdown, so this broader coverage claim is not grounded.
The opening paragraph is 38 words, below the required 40-75 word answer-first target. It is close, but still misses the explicit AEO requirement.

Some H2 first paragraphs are slightly thin for extraction. “Per-stage cost detail” opens with a click-path and “this view,” rather than naming “The Stacktape per-stage cost detail view…” as a standalone concept.

The page uses ConsoleScreenshot components with screenshot paths not present in the provided source list. That may be acceptable if the docs system owns those screenshots, but the review cannot verify that the screenshots exist or match current UI.
mandatoryFixes: Revise unsupported provenance wording: replace “Both views pull from AWS Cost and Usage Reports” with a narrower source-grounded statement such as “The Costs per stack page reads generated cost reports, and the Console notes that reports are generated daily and may take time to appear.”

Correct or narrow the “Costs by project” section. Do not assert the chart ranks projects unless a source proves it; anchor the description to the visible gadget title and the data shape supported by the source.
Remove or qualify unsupported history claims such as “last 6 months” for the overview sparkline unless the source list includes the API/server code proving the returned month count.
Replace unsupported all-account coverage claims in the FAQ with what the source proves: the Costs per stack page displays attributed stack costs from the selected AWS account and selected month.

Remove ungrounded Console navigation details such as “from the Console sidebar” and “Click any budget” unless console route/menu source is provided; say the Cost per stack page and Budget Alerts panel navigate to the relevant Console routes only where the provided source shows `navigate(...)`.
Expand the first paragraph to 40-75 words while keeping it self-contained and answer-first.
optionalImprovements: Rename “Per-stage cost detail” to “Stage Cost Detail” and start the section with a self-contained paragraph naming the Stacktape view explicitly.
Add a compact comparison table for the three jobs the page already distinguishes: viewing costs, controlling costs, and reducing costs.

Add one short caveat near the top that Console cost values are for AWS infrastructure spend and are not a replacement for AWS-native billing reconciliation when finance needs invoice-level detail. Keep the claim cautious unless more billing source is provided.
Consider replacing “AWS Costs KPI” with the exact card label plus a one-sentence explanation of current-month anchoring, since that is one of the most important gotchas on the page.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the Console source for the overview KPI, cost widgets, budget status panel, and costs-per-stack table. The main risks are over-specific claims about Console flows, data retention, AWS Cost and Usage Reports, and per-resource detail behavior that are not visible in the provided source files.

issues.statement: “Both views pull from AWS Cost and Usage Reports, updated daily.” and related FAQ wording: “AWS generates Cost and Usage Reports daily.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source shows TRPC cost queries and a no-data message saying cost reports are generated daily, but it does not identify AWS Cost and Usage Reports as the source for both dashboard views. The overview widgets call overview cost endpoints; the costs page calls awsAccountCostReports, but the source does not establish AWS CUR as the backing source for all views.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to what the source supports: “Cost reports are generated daily, and the Console cost views read from Stacktape cost-report endpoints.” Avoid naming AWS Cost and Usage Reports unless server/source evidence is provided.

evidence.quote: const costsQuery = trpc.overviewCostsByService.useQuery({ organizationId, month, year });
Please note that cost reports are generated daily and it can take a while before they are available.

issues.statement: “Clicking a row in the Costs per stack table opens a per-resource cost breakdown for that specific stage. This view shows each individual resource within the stage — its name, type, and cost for the selected month...”
issues.reason: The costs page source only shows row navigation to a project/stage costs route. The provided sources do not include the destination page implementation, so claims about columns, totals, per-resource rows, and month filtering in that destination are unsupported.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a narrower statement: “Clicking a row navigates to that project and stage’s costs page.” Link to `/managing-costs/per-resource-breakdown` for details instead of describing the missing page behavior.

issues.statement: “Do costs include all AWS services or only Stacktape-managed resources? The dashboards show all AWS costs in the connected account, including services not managed by Stacktape. However, the per-stack breakdown only attributes costs to resources that Stacktape has tagged...”
issues.reason: The provided source does not define whether overview dashboards include all AWS account costs, how untagged costs are handled, or whether per-stack attribution is tag-based. The costs page source only reads `breakdown.attributed.stacks`, which supports attributed stack data but not the broader account-wide and tagging claims.
issues.suggestedFix: Say only that the costs-per-stack table displays attributed stack costs from the selected connected AWS account. Avoid claims about untagged resources, AWS Support fees, external Route 53 zones, or tag mechanics unless server-side cost attribution source is provided.

issues.statement: “A sparkline below the card shows the last 6 months of total spend...” and “The organization overview's cost trend sparkline shows the last 6 months of totals.”
issues.reason: The OverviewPage source maps whatever months are returned by `overviewCostTrend`; it does not show that the endpoint returns exactly six months or that the values are monthly totals. The draft’s 6-month claim is not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more cautiously: “A sparkline below the card shows recent cost trend data returned by the Console.” Omit the exact month count unless the server endpoint source confirms it.

issues.statement: “Time range — select any month from the current month back through the previous calendar year, providing roughly 24 months of history.”
issues.reason: The source builds month options from the current month back to January of the current year, then all 12 months of the previous year. That is between 13 and 24 selectable months depending on the current month, not always roughly 24 months.
issues.suggestedFix: Use exact source-grounded wording: “The time range selector includes each month so far in the current year plus all 12 months of the previous year.”

issues.statement: “Click any budget to navigate to the budgets management page. When no budgets are configured, the panel shows a prompt to set one up.”
issues.reason: The source supports clicking budget rows and navigating to `/budgets`, and supports a no-budget message. It does not show a setup prompt; the exact empty state text is “No budget alerts configured.”
issues.suggestedFix: Change the empty-state sentence to: “When no budgets are configured, the panel says ‘No budget alerts configured.’”

issues.statement: “The Stacktape Console automatically attributes costs to your specific projects and stages, so you can see per-stack spend without manually configuring AWS Cost Allocation Tags or building custom Cost Explorer reports.”
issues.reason: The provided source shows per-stack attributed cost data in the Console, but it does not support the operational claim that users do not need to configure AWS Cost Allocation Tags or Cost Explorer reports. That is an implementation/setup claim outside the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the comparison narrower: “The Stacktape Console presents attributed per-stack spend by project, stage, and region, while AWS Billing shows account-level billing data.”

issues.statement: “Hover over any bar to see the exact amount and project name.”
issues.reason: The ApexCharts tooltip custom renderer shows formatted currency and item name, but the provided source does not prove the tooltip is triggered specifically by hover. This is likely true for ApexCharts, but the claim is a UI interaction detail inferred from the chart library rather than directly visible in the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Either omit the hover sentence or phrase it less specifically: “The chart tooltip displays the formatted amount and label for each bar.”

The organization overview description is largely grounded: the time-range tabs drive deployment statistics, while the AWS Costs KPI uses the current month and previous-month comparison.
The Costs per stack table columns, default sort column, and 10-row pagination are supported by the CostsAndUsagePage source.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the two Console source files, but it overstates several Console behaviors and cost-report details that are not present in the provided source. The most important fixes are the selectable-month range, unsupported per-resource drill-down details, unsupported refresh/update timing, and claims about account-wide or untagged AWS costs.

issues.statement: “Time range — select any month from the current month back through the previous calendar year, providing roughly 24 months of history.” Similar “~24-month range” and FAQ wording appears later.
issues.reason: The source builds the selector from months in the current year through the current month plus all 12 months from the previous year. reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong source grounding for Overview page sections — trend label table, 'Other' grouping at 10 items, time-range-controls-deploys-only caveat, and Budget Alerts panel all verified against OverviewPage.tsx.
Billing separation note is prominently placed and correctly distinguishes AWS charges from Stacktape subscription fee, satisfying the section-level instruction.
Lead paragraph is self-contained and within the 40–75 word target.
Trend-label table is an excellent use of tabular format for an enumerated set of UI states — directly reduces confusion for first-time users.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-behavior, comparison) and reaches 10 entries without obvious padding.
'AWS Costs by Project' vs 'AWS Costs by Resource' distinction is explained clearly and the difference matters practically — good decision-support content.
Costs per stack column table is accurate against the ElevatedTable columnHeaders in source.
Page correctly omits <ApiReference> — this is a Console UI page, not a resource-config page, so the omission is appropriate.
problems: Per-stage cost detail section ('Per-stage cost detail' H2) invents specifics not present in either provided source file: three specific column names ('Resource name', 'Type', 'Costs'), 'cost shown to four decimal places', 'total displayed at top of table', 'paginates at 15 rows per page', and 'sorts by cost descending by default'. The per-stage page source (routes to /projects/.../costs) is not in the provided source files — these are fabricated details that could actively mislead a user who opens the actual page.

The Warning callout and FAQ Q4 claim costs 'may take up to a day to appear'. The source no-data message says 'it can take a while before they are available' — the 'up to a day' precision is not in the source and should be softened to match.

The 'AWS Costs by Project' chart label vs the underlying data is slightly mismatched: the source component is CostsByProjectChart using trpc.overviewCostsByService and processes a 'services' list — the word 'Project' appears only in the gadget title. The doc confidently calls it a project-level breakdown without noting that the grouping key comes from the service name field, which could confuse users who see service-level names in the UI.

FAQ Q8 ('How far back can I view cost history?') and multiple other answer paragraphs repeat verbatim detail from the main body prose (the 13-to-24 selectable months logic). While some repetition is acceptable, three separate answer paragraphs quote the identical time-range logic — this is padding rather than additive content.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or heavily caveat the 'Per-stage cost detail' section. The source file for the per-stage page (/projects/.../costs) was not provided, and the section invents column names, pagination count (15 rows), decimal precision (four decimal places), sort order, and total placement. Replace the fabricated specifics with a single sourced sentence: 'Clicking a row in the Costs per stack table navigates to a per-resource cost breakdown for that stage.' Add a note that the exact columns and layout are visible in the Console. Do not invent column names, row counts, or decimal formats that cannot be verified.

Soften the 'up to a day' claim in the Warning callout and FAQ Q4 to match the source text. The source no-data message says 'it can take a while before they are available' — rewrite as 'reports are generated daily and may not be available immediately after a new deployment.'
optionalImprovements: Add a brief note that 'AWS Costs by Project' groups by the Stacktape service/project name as attributed by AWS Cost and Usage Reports — this explains why a user might see service-level names rather than their Stacktape project name in edge cases.

Consolidate the time-range description (current year months + previous year 12 months) into a single place instead of repeating it verbatim in the main body, per-stage section, and FAQ Q8. Reference back to the first mention instead of restating.

The <ConsoleScreenshot> components reference paths (/static/screenshots/console-overview-cost-kpis.png and /static/screenshots/console-costs-per-stack.png) that should be verified to exist before publishing — if the screenshots do not exist the component may render broken images.

FAQ Q5 ('What is the difference between AWS Costs by Project and AWS Costs by Resource?') is the most useful of the ten and would benefit from a direct link to the organization overview section above rather than the bare text description.

Consider adding a note about the dashboard's grid being customizable — the source shows a DashboardGrid with configurable gadgets — so users know they can rearrange or remove panels if their org dashboard looks different from the screenshots.

strengths: Correctly documents the non-obvious behavior that cost data on the overview is always the current calendar month and is independent of the 7d/14d/30d/90d/6m/1y time range selector — this is directly grounded in the source (useCostsData uses `new Date()` for month/year, not the timeRange state).
The trend label table with all four variants (percentage, Flat, New spend, No spend) accurately mirrors the conditional logic in the source, including the 0.1% threshold for Flat.
Budget Alerts panel description (name, scope, threshold, alertAtPercentages, navigation to /budgets, empty state text) is precisely grounded in BudgetStatusPanel source.
Costs per stack section correctly sources the column headers ('Project name', 'Stage', 'Region', 'Month-to-date costs'), pagination at 10 rows, and the exact no-data message from ElevatedTable.
The getSelectableMonths() analysis is correct: (thisMonth+1) current-year months + 12 prior-year months = 13 to 24 selectable months. The draft states this range accurately.
Clear upfront separation of viewing vs. alerting vs. optimization with targeted cross-links — directly addresses the managing-costs section instruction.
Billing note distinguishing AWS charges from Stacktape subscription is prominent and correctly placed.
problems: UNGROUNDED CLAIMS — Per-stage cost detail section: The source file for the per-stage cost page (/projects/{name}/{stage}/costs) was not provided. The draft asserts three specific columns ('Resource name', 'Type', 'Costs'), costs displayed 'to four decimal places', pagination 'at 15 rows per page', and 'sorts by cost descending by default'. None of these are in the provided source files. An engineer who reads these specifics and finds different behavior will lose trust in the docs.

UNGROUNDED CLAIM — 'sorts by cost descending by default' for the Costs per stack table: The source sets defaultSortColumnIndex={3} on ElevatedTable but the component source is not provided, so 'descending' is an assumption, not a sourced fact.

ACTIVE account filter omission: The CostsAndUsagePage source filters connected AWS accounts to only those where `acc.state === 'ACTIVE'`. The docs say only 'your connected AWS accounts' without noting inactive accounts are excluded, which matters when an engineer wonders why an account is missing from the dropdown.

KPI subtitle format imprecision: The source sets subtitle={`This month (${costsData.trendLabel} vs last)`}. The draft says the subtitle 'reads "This month" and includes the trend label for context', which understates the actual format. A reader expecting to see just 'This month' will be confused by the '(+12% vs last)' suffix.

Ambiguous 'AWS Costs by Project' framing: The chart's data query is overviewCostsByService, the internal data variable is named `services`, yet the draft describes it as grouping by application or project. If the AWS Cost and Usage Report data actually groups by AWS service type rather than Stacktape project, the docs will mislead engineers trying to cross-reference with their AWS bill.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or caveat all specific claims in the 'Per-stage cost detail' section that are not in the provided source files: the three column names ('Resource name', 'Type', 'Costs'), 'four decimal places', '15 rows per page', and 'sorts by cost descending by default'. Replace with what IS grounded: clicking a Costs per stack row navigates to a per-resource cost view for that stage at /projects/{name}/{stage}/costs. Either note that the specifics of that view are not covered here, or obtain and read the source file for that page before documenting it.
optionalImprovements: Add a note in the AWS account filter description that only accounts in ACTIVE state appear in the dropdown — sourced from `connectedAwsAccounts.filter(acc => acc.state === 'ACTIVE')`. Saves engineers a support question when a recently connected account doesn't appear.
Show the exact subtitle template 'This month (+X% vs last)' rather than vaguely saying 'includes the trend label', so engineers know what to look for in the UI.

Clarify what 'AWS Costs by Project' actually groups by — the internal query is overviewCostsByService and the data key is `services`. If this groups by Stacktape project name, confirm it; if it groups by AWS service category, rename the description to match.
The defaultSortColumnIndex={3} claim is correct, but drop the word 'descending' unless the ElevatedTable source confirms the default sort direction, to avoid asserting ungrounded behavior.
ConsoleScreenshot components reference screenshot paths that are not verified as existing — consider noting these are illustrative or confirm the paths are published before using them.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Clear separation between viewing costs, budgets, per-resource breakdown, and optimization pages matches the cost-management section guidance.

Opening paragraph is self-contained and in the right length range, and the page quickly clarifies that AWS infrastructure costs are billed to the user's AWS account separately from Stacktape subscription fees.
The page is easy to scan: short H2s, useful tables, concrete UI labels from the source, and a broad FAQ that covers Stacktape-specific, AWS-cost, and comparison questions.

The organization overview and Costs per stack sections capture several grounded details from source: current-month cost KPI, time range tabs affecting deployment stats, top-10 project cost chart with Other grouping, active AWS account selector, monthly selector, 10-row pagination, default sort by cost, and daily report delay messaging.
problems: The per-stage cost detail section is largely ungrounded by the provided source files. The source only shows row navigation to `/projects/{project}/{stage}/costs`; it does not show the destination page contents, columns, total, four-decimal formatting, 15-row pagination, or default sort behavior.

Several Console workflow claims go beyond the provided source, including that the Costs per stack page is accessible from the Console sidebar and that the per-stage detail view has the same month list as the Costs per stack page.

The draft repeatedly says AWS Cost and Usage Reports are the data source. The source exposes `awsAccountCostReports` and daily report messaging, but the provided files do not explicitly name AWS Cost and Usage Reports.

The FAQ answer claiming Stacktape adds no markup to AWS costs is stronger than the provided source. The sources and page instructions support saying AWS bills infrastructure costs directly and Stacktape subscription fees are separate, but not a general no-markup claim.

The AWS Billing console comparison claims project-level attribution without manual AWS Cost Allocation Tags or custom Cost Explorer reports. That may be true, but it is not grounded in the provided source files.

The Costs per stack filter description says users can compare spend across different AWS environments. The source filters active connected AWS accounts and labels the selector `AWS account`; it does not define those accounts as environments.

Some H2 first paragraphs are below the 40-75 word target or rely on surrounding context less than ideal, especially the FAQ being fine but `Per-stage cost detail` opening with a click path whose destination behavior is unsupported.

The page is practical for reading dashboards, but it cannot fully help readers act because the most actionable per-resource destination is described from missing source rather than verified Console source.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the `Per-stage cost detail` section with only what the provided source supports, or add the actual source file for `/projects/:project/:stage/costs`. With current sources, say that clicking a Costs per stack row navigates to that project and stage's costs page, but do not describe its table columns, totals, decimal formatting, pagination, or sorting.
Remove or narrow unsupported Console navigation claims. Do not say `accessible from the Console sidebar` unless a sidebar route/source file is provided.
Narrow `AWS Cost and Usage Reports` claims to source-grounded wording such as `cost reports are generated daily` unless a source file explicitly names AWS Cost and Usage Reports.

Rewrite the no-markup FAQ answer to the grounded fact: AWS infrastructure costs are billed directly to the user's AWS account, and Stacktape's subscription fee is separate and not shown in these dashboards.

Remove the unsupported AWS Billing console comparison about cost allocation tags and custom Cost Explorer reports, or rephrase as a cautious decision note without claiming Stacktape avoids those AWS-native setup steps.
Revise account wording to `active connected AWS accounts`, matching the source filter `acc.state === 'ACTIVE'`.
optionalImprovements: Add a small comparison table near the top with `Viewing cost`, `Controlling cost`, and `Reducing cost`, linking to dashboards, budgets, and optimization pages. The current prose says this, but a table would be more extractable.

In the Costs per stack section, mention that the default selected month is the current month because `getSelectableMonths()` returns the current month first and state initializes from `monthOptions[0]`.
Clarify that the organization overview cost charts show `No data` when no cost data is returned; this is grounded in both chart components and helps readers understand empty states.
Shorten a few FAQ questions or answers that restate earlier content, especially the overlap between newly deployed stack delays and update frequency.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the two provided Console source files, but it overstates several Console behaviors that are not visible in the provided source. The main blockers are unsupported claims about AWS Cost and Usage Reports, per-stage resource cost details, per-resource navigation, and exact Console interactions.

issues.statement: “Clicking a row in the Costs per stack table opens a per-resource cost breakdown for that specific stage. This view shows each resource within the stage — its name, type, and cost for the selected month…” and the related table/pagination/sorting claims in “Per-stage cost detail.”
issues.reason: The provided CostsAndUsagePage source only shows navigation to a project/stage costs route. It does not include the implementation of that destination page, so the draft’s claims about per-resource columns, totals, 15-row pagination, sorting, four-decimal formatting, and matching month selector are not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow this section to what the source proves: clicking a Costs per stack row navigates to `/projects/${projectOrServiceName}/${stage}/costs`. Omit detailed per-resource table behavior unless the source for that page is provided.

issues.statement: “Both views use data from AWS Cost and Usage Reports” and “Reports are generated daily from AWS Cost and Usage Reports.”
issues.reason: The provided source mentions cost reports and daily generation, but it does not identify AWS Cost and Usage Reports as the data source. That may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the supplied files.

issues.statement: “Costs per stack page — accessible from the Console sidebar.”

issues.statement: “Clicking a row [in Budget Alerts] navigates to the Console budgets page.”
issues.reason: The source shows the click handler navigates to `/budgets`, but the actual clickable element is each rendered budget item, not a table row. The draft describes a row/table interaction that is not present in the source.

issues.statement: “Hover over any bar to see the exact amount and name” for the AWS Costs by Project chart.
issues.reason: The tooltip source formats the total after rounding chart data to two decimals. Calling it the “exact amount” is too strong for the implementation shown.

issues.statement: “How is this different from the AWS Billing console? … without manually configuring AWS Cost Allocation Tags or building custom Cost Explorer reports.”

issues.statement: “No. The costs shown in the dashboards are your actual AWS charges, billed directly to your AWS account.”

issues.statement: “AWS Costs by Resource breaks down current-month spend by AWS resource type.”
issues.reason: The provided OverviewPage source shows the AWS Costs by Resource chart uses `trpc.overviewCostsByResourceType`, but unlike the project chart and KPI it does not pass an explicit month/year from the client. The source does not make the current-month scope visible for this chart.
issues.suggestedFix: Write “breaks down spend by AWS resource type” unless server-side source confirms the current-month window.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly grounded in the provided Console source and generated page summary. I found only minor accuracy issues around the exact trend-label semantics and one unsupported navigation-location detail.

issues.statement: The `Flat` trend label means “Less than 0.1% change in either direction.”
issues.reason: The Console code labels the trend as `Flat` when the percent change is not greater than 0.1 and not less than -0.1, so exactly +0.1% or -0.1% is also shown as `Flat`.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the meaning to “0.1% change or less in either direction” or “between -0.1% and +0.1%, inclusive.”

issues.statement: The `New spend` trend label means “Spend this month but no previous-month data to compare.”
issues.reason: The Console code sets `New spend` whenever current-month total is nonzero and `previousMonthTotal` is not greater than zero. That can mean the previous month had zero spend, not necessarily that previous-month data is missing.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the meaning to “Spend this month with a previous-month total of zero.”
